data.title: China Lesso Group Holdings (HKG:2128) shareholders notch a 75% return over 1 year, yet earnings have been shrinking
data.description:
These days it's easy to simply buy an index fund, and your returns should (roughly) match the market. But you can significantly boost your returns by picking above-average stocks. For example, the China Lesso Group Holdings Limited (HKG:2128) share price is up 66% in the last 1 year, clearly besting the market return of around 31% (not including dividends). That's a solid performance by our standards! In contrast, the longer term returns are negative, since the share price is 37% lower than it was three years ago.

After a strong gain in the past week, it's worth seeing if longer term returns have been driven by improving fundamentals.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

To quote Buffett, 'Ships will sail around the world but the Flat Earth Society will flourish. There will continue to be wide discrepancies between price and value in the marketplace...' One imperfect but simple way to consider how the market perception of a company has shifted is to compare the change in the earnings per share (EPS) with the share price movement.

Over the last twelve months, China Lesso Group Holdings actually shrank its EPS by 12%.

Given the share price gain, we doubt the market is measuring progress with EPS. Therefore, it seems likely that investors are putting more weight on metrics other than EPS, at the moment.

Unfortunately China Lesso Group Holdings' fell 11% over twelve months. So the fundamental metrics don't provide an obvious explanation for the share price gain.

You can see below how earnings and revenue have changed over time (discover the exact values by clicking on the image).

We consider it positive that insiders have made significant purchases in the last year. Even so, future earnings will be far more important to whether current shareholders make money. So it makes a lot of sense to check out what analysts think China Lesso Group Holdings will earn in the future (free profit forecasts).

Advertisement

What About Dividends?

When looking at investment returns, it is important to consider the difference between total shareholder return (TSR) and share price return. The TSR is a return calculation that accounts for the value of cash dividends (assuming that any dividend received was reinvested) and the calculated value of any discounted capital raisings and spin-offs. So for companies that pay a generous dividend, the TSR is often a lot higher than the share price return. We note that for China Lesso Group Holdings the TSR over the last 1 year was 75%, which is better than the share price return mentioned above. The dividends paid by the company have thusly boosted the total shareholder return.

A Different Perspective

It's good to see that China Lesso Group Holdings has rewarded shareholders with a total shareholder return of 75% in the last twelve months. And that does include the dividend. There's no doubt those recent returns are much better than the TSR loss of 8% per year over five years. This makes us a little wary, but the business might have turned around its fortunes. I find it very interesting to look at share price over the long term as a proxy for business performance. But to truly gain insight, we need to consider other information, too. To that end, you should learn about the 2 warning signs we've spotted with China Lesso Group Holdings (including 1 which makes us a bit uncomfortable) .

China Lesso Group Holdings is not the only stock that insiders are buying. For those who like to find lesser know companies this free list of growing companies with recent insider purchasing, could be just the ticket.

Please note, the market returns quoted in this article reflect the market weighted average returns of stocks that currently trade on Hong Kong exchanges.

data.title: Broadcom’s Microsoft Maia Role Highlights AI Growth And Valuation Tension
data.description:
• Broadcom is supplying custom chips that support Microsoft's new Maia AI accelerator for hyperscale data centers.
  • The company reports a record AI focused order backlog alongside expanded partnerships with large technology customers.
  • These developments highlight Broadcom's role in AI infrastructure hardware and related networking solutions.

Broadcom, listed as NasdaqGS:AVGO, is at the center of high performance networking and custom silicon that large cloud providers rely on. The shares last closed at $332.79, with a 1 year return of 61.9% and a 5 year gain of 675.7%. Those figures show how closely the stock has been tied to demand for advanced chips and data center capacity.

For investors watching AI infrastructure, the Maia chip work with Microsoft and an elevated AI specific backlog highlight Broadcom as a key supplier to hyperscale customers. As more workloads shift to AI heavy architectures, attention is likely to remain on how Broadcom manages these relationships and commitments in terms of revenue visibility and product breadth.

data.title: How Investors May Respond To Canadian National Railway (TSX:CNR) Freight Recession Cost Cuts And New CCO
data.description:
• Canadian National Railway recently launched a CA$75,000,000 cost reduction program, including management cuts and lower 2026 capital spending, to adjust to a freight recession and softer volumes.
  • The company has also installed a new Chief Commercial Officer to sharpen sales execution and boost utilization of its continent-spanning rail network and intermodal assets.
  • Next, we’ll examine how this cost reduction drive and leadership change could influence Canadian National Railway’s broader investment narrative.

The end of cancer? These 29 emerging AI stocks are developing tech that will allow early identification of life changing diseases like cancer and Alzheimer's.

Advertisement

What Is Canadian National Railway's Investment Narrative?

To own Canadian National Railway, you really need to believe in the resilience of a core North American freight infrastructure business that combines a continent-spanning network, high margins and disciplined capital allocation through dividends and buybacks. The recent CA$75,000,000 cost reduction plan and trimmed 2026 capex budget slot directly into that thesis: they are a response to a freight recession and softer volumes, and aim to protect profitability rather than to reset the company’s direction. Near term, the most important catalysts still look tied to any stabilization in freight demand and the new Chief Commercial Officer’s ability to lift network utilization and revenue quality, while the biggest risks remain high leverage, slower revenue growth than the broader Canadian market and questions around governance and pay, given elevated CEO compensation and mixed recent earnings trends.

But there is one risk around debt and demand sensitivity that investors should not overlook. Canadian National Railway's shares are on the way up, but could they be overextended? Uncover how much higher they are than fair value.

Exploring Other Perspectives

Eleven Simply Wall St Community fair values range from CA$119.59 to CA$155.05, reflecting wide opinion. Set this against CN’s freight recession response and cost cuts, then consider how demand risk could still influence outcomes.

data.title: Commvault Systems Q3 Margins Compressed To 7.3% Net Margin Challenge Bullish Growth Narrative
data.description:
Commvault Systems (CVLT) opened its Q3 2026 scorecard with total revenue of US$313.8 million and basic EPS of US$0.40, alongside net income excluding extra items of US$17.8 million. This gives investors a fresh read on how the business is scaling. The company has seen quarterly revenue move from US$262.6 million in Q3 2025 to US$313.8 million in Q3 2026, while basic EPS shifted from US$0.25 to US$0.40 over the same period. This sets the backdrop for a discussion that now centers on how margins are holding up as growth plays through the income statement.

See our full analysis for Commvault Systems.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings print lines up against the widely followed growth and profitability narratives that have built up around Commvault over the past year.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

Margins Squeezed, Even As Trailing Net Margin Sits At 7.3%

  • Over the last 12 months, Commvault’s trailing net income excluding extra items was US$87.001 million on US$1.147b of revenue. This works out to a 7.3% net margin compared with 19.7% a year earlier.
  • Bears focus on that margin step down, and the recent quarterly pattern gives them material support:
    • Q3 2026 net income excluding extra items of US$17.782 million on US$313.832 million of revenue is below the prior trailing net margin level. Earlier quarters like Q4 2025 showed US$30.993 million on US$275.039 million.
    • This sequence backs the bearish concern that profitability has been tighter over the last year, even as revenue moved from US$262.63 million in Q3 2025 to US$313.832 million in Q3 2026.

Forecast Earnings Growth Of 26.9% Versus Recent EPS Fluctuations

  • On a trailing twelve month basis, basic EPS is US$1.97, built from quarterly EPS figures that ranged from US$0.25 in Q3 2025 up to US$0.70 in Q4 2025 and then US$0.40 in Q3 2026, alongside an earnings growth forecast of about 26.9% per year.
  • Supporters of the bullish view lean on those growth forecasts, but the recent EPS pattern keeps the discussion grounded:
    • Consensus narrative points to earnings expected to grow faster than the wider US market at roughly 26.9% per year, while the last four reported quarters show EPS moving between US$0.25 and US$0.70 rather than following a straight line.
    • This combination heavily supports the bullish case for stronger long run earnings power, yet the quarter to quarter swings in reported EPS remind you that the path to that forecast is not perfectly smooth.
To see how this mix of fast earnings forecasts and choppy quarterly EPS feeds into the bigger story, analysts’ full narrative is a useful next step. 📊 Read the full Commvault Systems Consensus Narrative.

Premium Valuation With 49x P/E And US$168.83 Price Target

  • The stock trades on a trailing P/E of 49x compared with a 30x P/E for the US software industry and about 45.2x for peers. The current share price of US$89.13 sits above a cited DCF fair value of roughly US$78.96 and below analysts’ implied price target of US$168.83.
  • Critics highlight that premium as a key risk, yet the data also show why some investors still see room for upside:
    • On one hand, the 49x P/E and price above the DCF fair value of about US$78.96 back the bearish argument that the shares are richly priced against both fundamentals and industry averages.
    • On the other hand, analyst targets implying a move from US$89.13 to around US$168.83, together with the 26.9% earnings growth forecast and 10.8% revenue growth forecast, describe why some investors are comfortable paying above both the DCF fair value and sector P/E.

Next Steps

Don't just look at this quarter; the real story is in the long-term trend. We've done an in-depth analysis on Commvault Systems's growth and its valuation to see if today's price is a bargain. Add the company to your watchlist or portfolio now so you don't miss the next big move.

See What Else Is Out There

Commvault’s compressed net margins and choppy quarterly EPS, alongside a 49x P/E above its DCF fair value, leave some investors questioning the price they are paying.

If paying up for that kind of earnings volatility feels uncomfortable, use our these 881 undervalued stocks based on cash flows to quickly focus on companies where current prices look more aligned with underlying fundamentals.

data.title: Kimberly Clark (KMB) Margin Compression Reinforces Bearish Narrative On Earnings Quality
data.description:
Kimberly-Clark (KMB) has wrapped up FY 2025 with fourth quarter revenue of $4,080 million and basic EPS of US$1.15, setting a clear marker for how the year closed out on both the top and bottom line. The company has seen quarterly revenue move between $4,054 million and $4,163 million through 2025, while EPS ranged from US$1.01 to US$1.40 over the same stretch, giving investors a focused view of how sales and earnings have tracked into the latest print and what that might mean for margins.

See our full analysis for Kimberly-Clark.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to assess how this earnings profile lines up against the widely held narratives about Kimberly-Clark's growth, risks, and profitability.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

Margins Under Pressure At 9.9%

  • On a trailing 12 month basis, Kimberly-Clark reports a 9.9% net profit margin, compared with 12.7% a year earlier. This means less profit is being kept from each dollar of the US$16.4b in revenue.
  • Bears often worry that cost pressures will erode profitability. The move from a 12.7% to 9.9% margin gives that cautious view some backing, although quarterly net income excluding extra items held between US$336 million and US$464 million in FY 2025, which shows earnings are still substantial even with the lower margin.

Dividend Coverage Risk At 5.01%

  • The stock currently offers a 5.01% dividend yield, but that payout is flagged as not well covered by either earnings or free cash flow. This stands out against trailing net income of US$1.6b and a 9.9% margin.
  • Critics highlight this weak coverage as a key bearish point. The data supports that concern when you pair a high dividend yield with comments about a high level of debt, because both together can limit how much flexibility the company has if earnings or cash flow come under pressure.
Stay on top of how the income story evolves around these coverage and debt flags before you commit fresh capital. 🐻 Kimberly-Clark Bear Case

P/E Of 20.6x Versus Rich DCF Value

  • Kimberly-Clark trades on a 20.6x P/E ratio, slightly below the 21.4x peer average but above the 16.9x Global Household Products industry average. The provided DCF fair value of US$660.77 sits very far above the current US$100.64 share price.
  • Supporters of a more bullish view point out that analysts expect earnings to grow about 29.8% per year and revenue about 16.1% per year. When you set those growth forecasts alongside a P/E that is close to peers and a DCF fair value that is many times the current price, it heavily supports the optimistic case that the market is assigning a much lower value than the cash flow model suggests.
If you want to see how that big gap between price and DCF value fits into the broader growth story, it is worth reading the full narrative. 📊 Read the full Kimberly-Clark Consensus Narrative.

Next Steps

Don't just look at this quarter; the real story is in the long-term trend. We've done an in-depth analysis on Kimberly-Clark's growth and its valuation to see if today's price is a bargain. Add the company to your watchlist or portfolio now so you don't miss the next big move.

See What Else Is Out There

Kimberly-Clark's weaker 9.9% net margin and flagged dividend coverage against its debt load raise questions about how resilient its payout might be if conditions tighten.

If that mix of pressure on margins and a stretched dividend makes you uneasy, use our solid balance sheet and fundamentals stocks screener (388 results) to focus on companies with sturdier finances and more room to protect shareholder returns.

data.title: Assessing CNA Financial (CNA) Valuation After Recent Share Price Moves And DCF Discount
data.description:
Advertisement

What CNA Financial’s Recent Returns Signal for Investors

CNA Financial (CNA) has drawn attention after its shares delivered a 1.6% move over the past day and around 1.4% over the past week, while posting a small decline over the past month.

See our latest analysis for CNA Financial.

At a share price of $47.02, CNA’s recent 1.6% one day and 1.4% seven day share price returns sit against a modest 30 day pullback, while its 3 year total shareholder return of 37.6% and 5 year total shareholder return of 73.4% point to steadier gains for long term holders.

If CNA’s move has you looking beyond insurers, this could be a good moment to scan healthcare stocks for other income and defensively positioned names in adjacent sectors.

With CNA trading around $47, a value score of 4, an estimated 26.1% intrinsic discount and a price target sitting lower at $44, you have to ask: is this a genuine mispricing or is the market already building in future growth?

Preferred P/E of 12.8x: Is it justified?

Based on a P/E of 12.8x at a last close of $47.02, CNA Financial looks slightly cheaper than its peer group but roughly in line with the broader US insurance space.

The P/E multiple compares the share price with earnings per share. For insurers this ratio gives a quick read on how the market is pricing current profitability relative to other options in the sector.

CNA is described as expensive when stacked directly against the US insurance industry average P/E of 12.7x. It screens as good value versus a peer average of 15.6x and an estimated fair P/E of 15.5x, which suggests the market is assigning a lower earnings multiple than the level our fair ratio work points to as reasonable.

Explore the SWS fair ratio for CNA Financial

Result: Price-to-earnings of 12.8x (ABOUT RIGHT)

However, you also have to weigh risks such as a possible re rating closer to the US insurance P/E of 12.7x and CNA’s recent 1 year return of 1.6%.

Find out about the key risks to this CNA Financial narrative.

Another View: What Our DCF Says About CNA

While the P/E of 12.8x suggests CNA is roughly in line with the broader US insurance group, our DCF model comes out quite differently. On those cash flow assumptions, CNA at $47.02 sits about 26.1% below an estimated fair value of $63.65, which presents a much lower implied valuation.

That kind of gap can point to either a potential opportunity or a signal that the cash flow inputs may be too optimistic, so the key question is which explanation you consider more reasonable.

data.title: Assessing NPK International (NPKI) Valuation After Riverwater Partners’ Infrastructure Buildout Investment News
data.description:

See our latest analysis for NPK International.

The latest share price of $13.65 sits alongside a 30 day share price return of 12.62% and a year to date share price return of 12.81%. The 1 year total shareholder return of 88.28% and very large 5 year total shareholder return indicate that momentum has been building despite short term pullbacks.

If news around grid expansion has you looking at infrastructure related themes, it could be a good moment to scan aerospace and defense stocks as another way to uncover potential ideas.

So, with NPK International posting an 88.28% 1 year total shareholder return and trading at a discount to the US$16.75 analyst price target, is there still an entry point here, or is the market already pricing in future growth?

With NPK International’s most followed narrative pointing to a fair value of $16.75 against a $13.65 share price, the story hinges on how durable its earnings and cash generation can be as infrastructure work continues.

Strong and flexible balance sheet with ample liquidity allows continued investment in fleet expansion, operational efficiency, and share repurchases, while also enabling potential strategic acquisitions. This supports both revenue growth and shareholder returns (EPS uplift from buybacks) and underpins the company's undervaluation relative to forward growth prospects.

data.title: Assessing Manchester United (MANU) Valuation After A Quiet Period Of Share Price Gains
data.description:
Advertisement

What Manchester United (MANU) shareholders may focus on now

With no single headline event setting the tone, Manchester United (MANU) shares have quietly moved higher in the past month, alongside a mixed return profile over the past 3 months and year.

See our latest analysis for Manchester United.

At a share price of $17.64, Manchester United’s recent 11.22% 1 month share price return builds on an 11.79% year to date gain. However, the 3 year total shareholder return of 21.81% and 1 year total shareholder return of 9.63% point to relatively modest momentum overall rather than a strong trend.

If this kind of move has you thinking about where else the market might be pricing in potential shifts in sentiment, it could be a good time to scan fast growing stocks with high insider ownership.

With Manchester United trading at $17.64, screening with a low value score of 2 and showing an estimated intrinsic discount of around 20%, you have to ask: is this a genuine opportunity, or is the market already factoring in future growth?

Preferred Price-to-Sales of 3.3x: Is it justified?

On a P/S of 3.3x, Manchester United screens as expensive relative to both its Entertainment peers and the level our fair value work suggests could be more balanced.

The P/S ratio compares the company’s market value to its annual revenue. At 3.3x, investors are paying a little over three times Manchester United’s $663.8m of sales for each share’s slice of the business.

For a company that is currently loss making, a higher P/S can signal that the market is comfortable focusing on revenue scale and future earnings potential rather than present profitability, but the data here tells a mixed story.

Manchester United’s 3.3x P/S stands at roughly double the US Entertainment industry average of 1.6x, and it is also above the peer average of 2.0x. Our fair P/S estimate of 2.2x sits noticeably lower than the current multiple, which points to a level the market could move toward if sentiment around growth, profitability or balance sheet strength cools.

Explore the SWS fair ratio for Manchester United

Result: Price-to-Sales of 3.3x (OVERVALUED)

However, you also have to keep an eye on risks like continued losses, with net income at a $40.992m loss, or any cooling in revenue growth, which is currently 8.11%.

Find out about the key risks to this Manchester United narrative.

Another view: DCF points in the opposite direction

While the 3.3x P/S ratio makes Manchester United look expensive, our DCF model presents a different picture. data.title: A Look At Cardinal Health (CAH) Valuation As Earnings And Analyst Upgrades Refocus Investor Attention
data.description:
Cardinal Health (CAH) heads into its February 5, 2026 earnings report with investor attention focused on revised forecasts for earnings per share and revenue, as well as expectations around its Pharmaceutical and Medical segment performance.

data.title: Does Cleveland-Cliffs’ POSCO Tie-Up Offset Analyst Caution on Its Balance Sheet Priorities (CLF)?
data.description:
• In recent days, Cleveland-Cliffs was downgraded from Buy to Neutral by Seaport Global Securities, even as the company announced a memorandum of understanding with South Korean steelmaker POSCO and added Edilson Camara to its Board of Directors.
  • This combination of a more cautious analyst stance alongside a potential POSCO partnership highlights the tension between balance-sheet concerns and operational opportunities in Cleveland-Cliffs’ story.
  • Next, we’ll examine how the analyst downgrade, against the backdrop of the POSCO memorandum of understanding, shapes Cleveland-Cliffs’ investment narrative.

Trump's oil boom is here - pipelines are primed to profit. Discover the 22 US stocks riding the wave.

Advertisement

What Is Cleveland-Cliffs' Investment Narrative?

To own Cleveland-Cliffs today, you need to be comfortable with a company that is unprofitable, highly leveraged and yet still priced by the market above consensus analyst fair value. The recent Seaport Global downgrade, coming just as the POSCO memorandum of understanding and a new director appointment were announced, neatly captures that tension. On one side, the POSCO framework could support volumes, costs and balance sheet flexibility, which matters for a business whose debt is not well covered by operating cash flow. On the other, the downgrade reinforces that equity dilution and debt servicing remain front-of-mind short term risks, even after a strong one-year share price rebound. Taken together, the news slightly tilts the near term story toward balance sheet execution rather than pure operational upside.

However, investors should be aware of how any future equity raise might affect their stake. Cleveland-Cliffs' shares have been on the rise but are still potentially undervalued by 45%. Find out what it's worth.

Exploring Other Perspectives

Six Simply Wall St Community fair value views span roughly US$13 to almost US$57 per share, so you are seeing very different expectations. Set against current losses and leverage concerns, that spread underlines why comparing multiple viewpoints before deciding on Cleveland-Cliffs’ prospects really matters.

data.title: Is It Time To Reconsider Range Resources (RRC) After Its Recent Share Price Recovery
data.description:
• If you have been wondering whether Range Resources is still good value after a long run in energy markets, this article will walk through what the current price could imply about the stock.
  • The shares most recently closed at US$36.69, with returns of 6.6% over the last 7 days, 3.9% over the last 30 days, 3.9% year to date, a 0.6% decline over 1 year and 54.0% over 3 years, which hints that expectations and perceived risk have been shifting over different time frames.
  • Recent coverage of Range Resources has focused on its position as a US natural gas and liquids producer, and how changing energy prices and supply expectations have influenced sentiment toward the stock. This context helps explain why shorter term returns look different to the longer term track record.
  • On our valuation checks, Range Resources scores a 4 out of 6. Next, we will look at what different valuation approaches say about that score, before finishing with a tool that can help you assess value in an even more practical way.

Find out why Range Resources's -0.6% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Range Resources Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a business could be worth today by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back to a single present value figure.

For Range Resources, the model uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on last twelve month free cash flow of about $492.5 million and projected free cash flows that extend out over the next decade. Analyst inputs cover the earlier years and Simply Wall St extrapolates the later period, with projected free cash flow for 2035 of about $746.0 million. All of these cash flows are in $ and are discounted back to today.

On this basis, the DCF model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of US$67.79 per share, compared with the recent share price of US$36.69. That difference indicates the stock is around 45.9% below the level suggested by the cash flow projections in this model.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Range Resources is undervalued by 45.9%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 887 more undervalued stocks based on cash flows.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Range Resources.

Approach 2: Range Resources Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies like Range Resources, the P/E ratio is a straightforward way to connect what you pay for each share with the earnings that support it. It gives you a quick sense of how much the market is paying for each dollar of profit.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends a lot on how investors see growth potential and risk. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can support a higher multiple, while lower expected growth or higher perceived risk usually goes with a lower multiple.

Range Resources currently trades on a P/E of 15.18x, compared with the Oil and Gas industry average of about 13.45x and a peer group average of 46.83x. Simply Wall St also calculates a proprietary “Fair Ratio” of 20.44x, which reflects factors such as earnings growth, profit margins, industry, company size and specific risks.

This Fair Ratio can be more useful than a simple peer or industry comparison because it looks at the company’s own characteristics rather than assuming all peers deserve similar multiples. Setting the current P/E of 15.18x against the Fair Ratio of 20.44x suggests the shares are trading below the level implied by those fundamentals.

Result: UNDERVALUED

P/E ratios tell one story, but what if the real opportunity lies elsewhere? Discover 1427 companies where insiders are betting big on explosive growth.

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Range Resources Narrative

Earlier we mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation, so let us introduce you to Narratives. A Narrative is simply writing the story you believe about a company and linking that story directly to the numbers behind it.

With a Narrative, you set out your view of Range Resources, including what you think is a reasonable fair value, how revenue and earnings might develop, and what profit margins could look like. You then connect that to a clear valuation instead of relying only on broad ratios or past returns.

On Simply Wall St, within the Community page used by millions of investors, Narratives are an easy tool that tie the company story to a financial forecast and then to a fair value. They update automatically when new information such as news or earnings is added, so your view is always linked to the latest data.

This can help inform your decision making by allowing you to compare your Narrative fair value with the current share price. One investor might see Range Resources as attractively priced, while another using more conservative revenue or margin assumptions might see it as fairly valued or even expensive.

Do you think there's more to the story for Range Resources? Head over to our Community to see what others are saying!

data.title: Can Analyst Optimism and Rising Capital Intensity Coexist in Moog’s (MOG.A) Investment Story?
data.description:
• Moog recently drew attention for a pattern of surpassing earnings estimates, with analysts becoming increasingly upbeat about its upcoming results.
  • Beneath this optimism, the company’s relatively modest long-term revenue growth and rising capital intensity raise questions about the efficiency of its expansion efforts.

What Is Moog's Investment Narrative?

For Moog to make sense in a portfolio, you need to buy into a story of steady, technically complex aerospace and defense exposure where execution quality matters more than rapid top line expansion. The recent pattern of earnings beats and bullish analyst sentiment slots neatly into that narrative, potentially reinforcing near term confidence in the company’s US$4.20 billion sales and US$10 EPS targets for fiscal 2026. At the same time, the share price has already run very hard over the past year, so any disappointment versus these heightened expectations could quickly refocus attention on slower long term revenue growth, rising capital intensity and the company’s high debt load. In other words, the news supports the bull case, but it also raises the bar for what “good enough” looks like next.

However, investors also need to weigh how rising capital intensity could pressure future flexibility. Despite retreating, Moog's shares might still be trading above their fair value and there could be some more downside. Discover how much.

data.title: Shareholder Proposals Put Starbucks Governance And Human Capital Under Spotlight
data.description:
• On 26 January 2026, multiple shareholder proposals were submitted to Starbucks (NasdaqGS:SBUX).
  • The proposals question the company’s governance practices, including board structure and voting requirements.
  • They also address human capital topics such as gender and healthcare benefits and treatment of religious organizations in employee programs.
  • Additional scrutiny focuses on Starbucks’ diagnostic partnerships and broader corporate policies.

For you as an investor, this matters because Starbucks is not just a coffee chain; it is a large global employer with extensive partner benefits programs and a widely held consumer brand. When shareholders push on governance and people policies, it can influence how the company manages risk, retains employees, and protects its brand with customers.

These proposals indicate that investors are paying close attention to how Starbucks runs its board, structures benefits, and makes values-based decisions. As this process unfolds, you may want to monitor how management and the board respond, how much support each proposal receives at the meeting, and whether any policy shifts are announced afterward.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Starbucks by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Starbucks.

Why Starbucks could be great value

The cluster of proposals zeroes in on how Starbucks designs its benefits, manages social and political sensitivities, and structures power at the top. Requests for reports on gender related pay and benefits, detransition healthcare coverage, diagnostic partnerships, and the treatment of religious charities in gift matching suggest that a vocal group of shareholders wants more transparency on financial, legal, and reputational risks tied to people policies and corporate values.

Advertisement

Starbucks narrative watch: human capital and brand in focus

For a company often associated with a values driven brand and a large frontline workforce, this type of investor activity can test the narrative that strong employee programs and brand affinity support long term performance. You may see debates sharpen between those who view these policies as core to Starbucks’ identity and those who frame them primarily as risk factors that need tighter board level oversight.

Key risks and rewards on the table

  • ⚠️ Governance structure concerns, including a proposal to separate the CEO and Chair roles, point to questions about board independence and oversight.
  • ⚠️ Calls for reviews of healthcare coverage, diagnostic partners, and charity exclusions highlight possible legal, operational, and reputational risk.
  • ⚠️ Existing flags around negative shareholders’ equity and dividend coverage mean governance and capital allocation choices are likely to be closely scrutinized together.
  • 🎁 Greater disclosure on compensation and benefits gaps, and clearer voting standards, could give investors more tools to assess long term human capital management.

What to watch next

From here, the key things to watch are how the board responds in its proxy materials, the level of support each proposal receives, and whether management signals any shifts in governance or benefits policies at upcoming events or in future reports. If you want to see how other investors are framing these issues and how they fit into the broader Starbucks story, you can check out what the community is saying.

data.title: Is It Time To Revisit Ameriprise Financial (AMP) After Its Recent Share Price Stumble?
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Ameriprise Financial at around US$500 per share still offers value or is starting to look expensive, you are not alone.
  • The stock has had a mixed run, with a 1.7% decline over the last 7 days, a 0.3% decline over 30 days, a 1.4% gain year to date, a 9.7% decline over 1 year, and stronger returns of 52.1% over 3 years and 169.1% over 5 years.
  • Recent headlines have focused on Ameriprise Financial's position in wealth management and financial planning, as investors consider how its business model responds to changing client needs and market conditions. This context around its core services helps frame how the market is currently thinking about risk, resilience, and long term growth potential for the stock.
  • Our valuation checks suggest Ameriprise Financial scores 5 out of 6 on our value framework, with a value score of 5. Next we will look at how different valuation approaches line up on the stock before finishing with a more complete way to think about its value.

Find out why Ameriprise Financial's -9.7% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Ameriprise Financial Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how much profit a company can generate over and above the return that equity investors typically require. Instead of focusing on cash flows, it starts with the book value of equity and asks how efficiently that equity is being used to generate earnings.

For Ameriprise Financial, the model uses a Book Value of $69.31 per share and a Stable EPS of $45.15 per share, based on weighted future Return on Equity estimates from 4 analysts. The implied Cost of Equity is $7.88 per share, which leaves an Excess Return of $37.28 per share. That gap is supported by an Average Return on Equity of 47.13%, applied to a Stable Book Value estimate of $95.81 per share, sourced from 5 analysts.

Putting these inputs together, the Excess Returns framework arrives at an intrinsic value of about $847.23 per share. Compared with a current share price around $500, this implies a 40.9% discount, which indicates that Ameriprise Financial is trading materially below this model’s estimate of value.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Excess Returns analysis suggests Ameriprise Financial is undervalued by 40.9%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 887 more undervalued stocks based on cash flows.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Ameriprise Financial.

Approach 2: Ameriprise Financial Price vs Earnings

For a profitable business like Ameriprise Financial, the P/E ratio is a useful shorthand for how much investors are currently paying for each dollar of earnings. It links directly to what you get as a shareholder today, the company’s earnings, without needing detailed cash flow forecasts.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends on how the market views growth potential and risk. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can support a higher multiple, while slower expected growth or higher risk usually point to a lower one.

Ameriprise currently trades on a P/E of 12.82x, which sits below both the Capital Markets industry average P/E of 24.41x and the broader peer average of 27.40x. Simply Wall St’s “Fair Ratio” for Ameriprise is 16.25x, a proprietary estimate of what the P/E might be given factors like the company’s earnings growth profile, its industry, profit margins, market cap and key risks.

This Fair Ratio aims to be more tailored than a simple peer or industry comparison, because it adjusts for company specific characteristics rather than assuming all firms should trade on similar multiples. Comparing 16.25x to the actual 12.82x suggests the current P/E sits below this fair value anchor.

Result: UNDERVALUED

P/E ratios tell one story, but what if the real opportunity lies elsewhere? Discover 1427 companies where insiders are betting big on explosive growth.

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Ameriprise Financial Narrative

Earlier we mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation, so let us introduce you to Narratives, a simple way to connect your view of Ameriprise Financial’s business to a financial forecast and then to a fair value estimate that you can compare with today’s share price.

A Narrative is your story behind the numbers, where you outline how you think revenue, earnings and margins might develop and the Simply Wall St platform turns that into a consistent forecast and fair value. All of this is visible on the Community page that millions of investors use.

Because Narratives sit on top of live data, they update automatically when fresh information like earnings or news is released. This means your view on whether Ameriprise Financial looks cheap or expensive relative to your fair value is always linked to current information.

For Ameriprise Financial, one investor might build a Narrative that supports a higher fair value based on strong wealth management economics. Another might set a lower fair value if they expect more modest client activity and profitability. Both views can sit side by side for you to compare with the current share price and decide what action, if any, makes sense for you.

data.title: Is Teleflex (TFX) Starting To Look Interesting After A 43% One Year Share Price Slide
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Teleflex at US$104.20 is starting to look interesting on value, you are not alone.
  • The share price has seen a 1.2% gain over the last 7 days, set against a 15.1% decline over 30 days and a 42.6% drop over the past year, which can change how the market prices both risk and opportunity.
  • Recent coverage has focused on Teleflex as a long established medical technology company, with investors weighing its position in the healthcare sector against ongoing share price weakness. That mix of sector interest and a 55.7% 3 year and 71.6% 5 year return decline is a key backdrop for thinking about what the stock might be worth today.
  • On Simply Wall St’s 6 point valuation checklist, Teleflex scores a 4 out of 6. Below, we break this down using several valuation methods, then finish with a different way of thinking about value that brings those methods together.

Find out why Teleflex's -42.6% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Teleflex Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model estimates what a company might be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back to today using an assumed rate of return. It is essentially asking what those future dollars are worth in present terms.

For Teleflex, the latest twelve month free cash flow is reported at $273.07 million. Analysts provide free cash flow estimates to 2026, with Simply Wall St extending those projections out to 2035 using a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model. The 2026 free cash flow estimate is $115.60 million, and the later years gradually step down from there based on the supplied projections.

After discounting all those projected cash flows back to today, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of $14.52 per share. Compared with the current share price of roughly $104.20, the DCF output suggests the stock is very expensive relative to the model, implying it is significantly overvalued on this basis alone.

Result: OVERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Teleflex may be overvalued by 617.6%. Discover 887 undervalued stocks or create your own screener to find better value opportunities.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Teleflex.

Approach 2: Teleflex Price vs Sales

For a company like Teleflex, where investors often focus on revenue across its product portfolio, the price to sales (P/S) ratio can be a useful way to think about what you are paying for each dollar of sales, especially when earnings are not the main reference point.

Growth expectations and risk still matter here. Higher expected revenue growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/S ratio, while slower growth or higher risk usually support a lower, more conservative multiple.

Teleflex is currently trading on a P/S of 1.44x. That sits below the Medical Equipment industry average P/S of 3.29x and also below the peer group average of 3.74x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Teleflex is 2.05x, which reflects a P/S level that the model suggests could be appropriate after weighing factors like earnings growth, profit margins, industry, market cap and company specific risks.

This Fair Ratio aims to be more tailored than a simple peer or industry comparison because it adjusts for Teleflex’s own characteristics rather than assuming all companies should trade at the same multiple. Compared with the current P/S of 1.44x, the Fair Ratio of 2.05x indicates that the shares appear undervalued on this measure.

Result: UNDERVALUED

P/S ratios tell one story, but what if the real opportunity lies elsewhere? Discover 1427 companies where insiders are betting big on explosive growth.

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Teleflex Narrative

Earlier we mentioned that there is an even better way to think about valuation, so let us introduce you to Narratives. Narratives let you set a clear story for Teleflex that sits behind the numbers such as your fair value, and your expectations for future revenue, earnings and margins.

A Narrative links what you believe about the business, for example its product pipeline, competitive position or risks, to a financial forecast and then to a fair value that you can easily compare with the current share price to guide your own investment decisions.

On Simply Wall St, Narratives sit inside the Community page and are designed to be simple to use. You can pick or adjust assumptions without building a full spreadsheet, and the platform then connects those assumptions to a live valuation that updates when new information such as earnings releases or company news is added.

For Teleflex, one investor might build a Narrative that results in a relatively low fair value, while another might use different revenue or margin assumptions and arrive at a much higher fair value. This shows how the same company can support very different yet clearly explained viewpoints.

data.title: Investor Optimism Abounds Senetas Corporation Limited (ASX:SEN) But Growth Is Lacking
data.description:
With a median price-to-sales (or "P/S") ratio of close to 2x in the Communications industry in Australia, you could be forgiven for feeling indifferent about Senetas Corporation Limited's (ASX:SEN) P/S ratio of 1.8x. However, investors might be overlooking a clear opportunity or potential setback if there is no rational basis for the P/S.

Trump has pledged to "unleash" American oil and gas and these 15 US stocks have developments that are poised to benefit.

Check out our latest analysis for Senetas

Advertisement

How Senetas Has Been Performing

While the industry has experienced revenue growth lately, Senetas' revenue has gone into reverse gear, which is not great. One possibility is that the P/S ratio is moderate because investors think this poor revenue performance will turn around. If not, then existing shareholders may be a little nervous about the viability of the share price.

Want the full picture on analyst estimates for the company? Then our free report on Senetas will help you uncover what's on the horizon.

How Is Senetas' Revenue Growth Trending?

Senetas' P/S ratio would be typical for a company that's only expected to deliver moderate growth, and importantly, perform in line with the industry.

In reviewing the last year of financials, we were disheartened to see the company's revenues fell to the tune of 11%. The last three years don't look nice either as the company has shrunk revenue by 23% in aggregate. Accordingly, shareholders would have felt downbeat about the medium-term rates of revenue growth.

Turning to the outlook, the next three years should generate growth of 6.9% each year as estimated by the one analyst watching the company. With the industry predicted to deliver 22% growth per year, the company is positioned for a weaker revenue result.

With this information, we find it interesting that Senetas is trading at a fairly similar P/S compared to the industry. It seems most investors are ignoring the fairly limited growth expectations and are willing to pay up for exposure to the stock. Maintaining these prices will be difficult to achieve as this level of revenue growth is likely to weigh down the shares eventually.

What Does Senetas' P/S Mean For Investors?

We'd say the price-to-sales ratio's power isn't primarily as a valuation instrument but rather to gauge current investor sentiment and future expectations.

When you consider that Senetas' revenue growth estimates are fairly muted compared to the broader industry, it's easy to see why we consider it unexpected to be trading at its current P/S ratio. When we see companies with a relatively weaker revenue outlook compared to the industry, we suspect the share price is at risk of declining, sending the moderate P/S lower. Circumstances like this present a risk to current and prospective investors who may see share prices fall if the low revenue growth impacts the sentiment.

Before you take the next step, you should know about the 1 warning sign for Senetas that we have uncovered.

If companies with solid past earnings growth is up your alley, you may wish to see this free collection of other companies with strong earnings growth and low P/E ratios.

data.title: Lumos Diagnostics Holdings Limited (ASX:LDX) Stocks Pounded By 27% But Not Lagging Industry On Growth Or Pricing
data.description:
Lumos Diagnostics Holdings Limited (ASX:LDX) shareholders won't be pleased to see that the share price has had a very rough month, dropping 27% and undoing the prior period's positive performance. Regardless, last month's decline is barely a blip on the stock's price chart as it has gained a monstrous 356% in the last year.

Although its price has dipped substantially, Lumos Diagnostics Holdings may still be sending strong sell signals at present with a price-to-sales (or "P/S") ratio of 9.1x, when you consider almost half of the companies in the Medical Equipment industry in Australia have P/S ratios under 6x and even P/S lower than 2x aren't out of the ordinary. Nonetheless, we'd need to dig a little deeper to determine if there is a rational basis for the highly elevated P/S.

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

Check out our latest analysis for Lumos Diagnostics Holdings

Advertisement

How Lumos Diagnostics Holdings Has Been Performing

Recent times have been advantageous for Lumos Diagnostics Holdings as its revenues have been rising faster than most other companies. The P/S is probably high because investors think this strong revenue performance will continue. However, if this isn't the case, investors might get caught out paying too much for the stock.

Keen to find out how analysts think Lumos Diagnostics Holdings' future stacks up against the industry? In that case, our free report is a great place to start.

Is There Enough Revenue Growth Forecasted For Lumos Diagnostics Holdings?

There's an inherent assumption that a company should far outperform the industry for P/S ratios like Lumos Diagnostics Holdings' to be considered reasonable.

If we review the last year of revenue growth, the company posted a worthy increase of 11%. The latest three year period has also seen a 6.6% overall rise in revenue, aided somewhat by its short-term performance. Accordingly, shareholders would have probably been satisfied with the medium-term rates of revenue growth.

Turning to the outlook, the next year should generate growth of 69% as estimated by the only analyst watching the company. With the industry only predicted to deliver 53%, the company is positioned for a stronger revenue result.

With this in mind, it's not hard to understand why Lumos Diagnostics Holdings' P/S is high relative to its industry peers. It seems most investors are expecting this strong future growth and are willing to pay more for the stock.

The Final Word

Lumos Diagnostics Holdings' shares may have suffered, but its P/S remains high. Using the price-to-sales ratio alone to determine if you should sell your stock isn't sensible, however it can be a practical guide to the company's future prospects.

As we suspected, our examination of Lumos Diagnostics Holdings' analyst forecasts revealed that its superior revenue outlook is contributing to its high P/S. Right now shareholders are comfortable with the P/S as they are quite confident future revenues aren't under threat. Unless these conditions change, they will continue to provide strong support to the share price.

Before you take the next step, you should know about the 2 warning signs for Lumos Diagnostics Holdings that we have uncovered.
